---
title: "Class 9: Candy Mini-Project"
author: "Matthew Huang (A17978767)"
format: pdf
---

## 1. Background
Your task is to explore their candy dataset to find out answers to these types of questions - but most of all your job is to have fun, learn by doing hands on data analysis, and hopefully make this type of analysis less frightening for the future! Let‚Äôs get started.

## 2. Importing Candy Data


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
candy_file <- "candy-data.csv"

candy = read.csv(candy_file, row.names=1)
head(candy)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
             chocolate fruity caramel peanutyalmondy nougat crispedricewafer
100 Grand            1      0       1              0      0                1
3 Musketeers         1      0       0              0      1                0

One quarter          0      0       0              0      0                0
Air Heads            0      1       0              0      0                0
Almond Joy           1      0       0              1      0                0
             hard bar pluribus sugarpercent pricepercent winpercent
100 Grand       0   1        0        0.732        0.860   66.97173
3 Musketeers    0   1        0        0.604        0.511   67.60294
One dime        0   0        0        0.011        0.116   32.26109

Air Heads       0   0        0        0.906        0.511   52.34146
```

## 2.1 What is in the dataset?

>Q1. How many different candy types are in this dataset? 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
nrow(candy)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 85
```


:::
:::



>Q2. How many fruity candy types are in the dataset? 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sum(candy$fruity == 1)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 38
```


:::
:::

## **Side-Note:** : the skimr::skim() function
There is a useful skim() function in the skimr package that can help give you a quick overview of a given dataset. Let‚Äôs install this package and try it on our candy data.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library("skimr")
skim(candy)
```

>Q6. Is there any variable/column that looks to be on a different scale to the majority of the other columns in the dataset?

The winpercent variable.

>Q7. What do you think a zero and one represent for the `candy$chocolate` column?

Zeros are for non-chocolate candies, and the ones are for chocolates. 

## 3. Exploratory Analysis
>Q8. Plot a histogram of `winpercent` values using both base R an ggplot2.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
hist(candy$winpercent,
     main = "Distribution of Candy Win Percent",
     xlab = "Win Percent",
     col = "blue")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class9MH_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-10-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


>Q9. Is the distribution of `winpercent` values symmetrical?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
summary(candy$winpercent)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
  22.45   39.14   47.83   50.32   59.86   84.18 
```


:::
:::


No, slightly right skewed. 

>Q10. Is the center of the distribution above or below 50%?

Below 50%.

>Q11. On average is chocolate candy higher or lower ranked than fruit candy?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
mean(candy$winpercent[candy$chocolate == 1])
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 60.92153
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
mean(candy$winpercent[candy$fruity == 1])
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 44.11974
```


:::
:::

On average, chocolate candies have higher winpercent values than fruity candies.

>Q12. Is this difference statistically significant?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
t.test(
  candy$winpercent[candy$chocolate == 1],
  candy$winpercent[candy$fruity == 1]
)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

	Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  candy$winpercent[candy$chocolate == 1] and candy$winpercent[candy$fruity == 1]
t = 6.2582, df = 68.882, p-value = 2.871e-08
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 11.44563 22.15795
sample estimates:
mean of x mean of y 
 60.92153  44.11974 
```


:::
:::

With the t-test yielding a p-value <0.05, the difference is statistically significant. 

## 4. Overall Candy Rankings

Let‚Äôs use the base R `order()` function together with `head()` to sort the whole dataset by `winpercent`. Or if you have been getting into the tidyverse and the *dplyr* package you can use the `arrange()` function together with `head()` to do the same thing and answer the following questions:

>Q13. What are the five least liked candy types in this set?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
head(candy[order(candy$winpercent), ], 5)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

Boston Baked Beans         0      0       0              1      0
Chiclets                   0      1       0              0      0
Super Bubble               0      1       0              0      0
Jawbusters                 0      1       0              0      0
                   crispedricewafer hard bar pluribus sugarpercent pricepercent
Nik L Nip                         0    0   0        1        0.197        0.976
Boston Baked Beans                0    0   0        1        0.313        0.511
Chiclets                          0    0   0        1        0.046        0.325
Super Bubble                      0    0   0        0        0.162        0.116
Jawbusters                        0    1   0        1        0.093        0.511
                   winpercent
Nik L Nip            22.44534
Boston Baked Beans   23.41782
Chiclets             24.52499
Super Bubble         27.30386
Jawbusters           28.12744
```


:::
:::

Nik L Nip, Boston Baked Beans, Chiclets, Super Bubble, and Jawbusters

```{.r .cell-code}
head(candy[order(candy$winpercent, decreasing = TRUE), ], 5)
```

```

                          pricepercent winpercent
```

>Q15. Make a first barplot of candy ranking based on `winpercent` values.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(candy) +
  aes(winpercent, rownames(candy)) +
  geom_col()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class9MH_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-16-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


>Q16. This is quite ugly, use the `reorder()` function to get the bars sorted by `winpercent?`


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(candy) +
  aes(winpercent, reorder(rownames(candy), winpercent)) +
  geom_col()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class9MH_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-17-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


## 4.0.1 Time to add some useful color

Let‚Äôs setup a color vector (that signifies candy type) that we can then use for some future plots. We start by making a vector of all black values (one for each candy). Then we overwrite chocolate (for chocolate candy), brown (for candy bars) and red (for fruity candy) values.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
my_cols=rep("black", nrow(candy))
my_cols[as.logical(candy$chocolate)] = "chocolate"
my_cols[as.logical(candy$bar)] = "yellow"
my_cols[as.logical(candy$fruity)] = "pink"
```
:::


Now let‚Äôs try our barplot with these colors. Note that we use `fill=my_cols` for `geom_col()`. Experiment to see what happens if you use `col=mycols`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(candy) + 
  aes(winpercent, reorder(rownames(candy),winpercent)) +
  geom_col(fill=my_cols) 
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class9MH_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-19-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


>Q17. What is the worst ranked chocolate candy?

Sixlets

>Q18. What is the best ranked fruity candy?

Starbursts

## 5. Taking a look at pricepercent

What about value for money? What is the best candy for the least money? One way to get at this would be to make a plot of `winpercent` vs the `pricepercent` variable. The `pricepercent` variable records the percentile rank of the candy‚Äôs price against all the other candies in the dataset. Lower values are less expensive and higher values are more expensive.

To this plot we will add text labels so we can more easily identify a given candy. There is a regular `geom_label()` that comes with ggplot2. However, as there are quite a few candies in our dataset lots of these labels will be overlapping and hard to read. To help with this we can use the `geom_text_repel()` function from the *ggrepel* package.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(ggrepel)

ggplot(candy) +
  aes(winpercent, pricepercent, label = rownames(candy)) +
  geom_point(col = my_cols) +
  geom_text_repel(col = my_cols, size = 3.3, max.overlaps = 5)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning: ggrepel: 65 unlabeled data points (too many overlaps). Consider
increasing max.overlaps
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class9MH_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-20-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


>Q19. Which candy type is the highest ranked in terms of `winpercent` for the least money - i.e. offers the most bang for your buck?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ord <- order(candy$pricepercent)
head(candy[ord, c(11,12)], n = 10)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                     pricepercent winpercent
Tootsie Roll Midgies        0.011   45.73675
Pixie Sticks                0.023   37.72234
Dum Dums                    0.034   39.46056
Fruit Chews                 0.034   43.08892
Strawberry bon bons         0.058   34.57899
Root Beer Barrels           0.069   29.70369
Sixlets                     0.081   34.72200
Hershey's Kisses            0.093   55.37545
Lemonhead                   0.104   39.14106
One dime                    0.116   32.26109
```


:::
:::


Tootsie Roll Midgies. 

>Q20. What are the top 5 most expensive candy types in the dataset and of these which is the least popular?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ord <- order(candy$pricepercent, decreasing = TRUE)
head(candy[ord, c(11,12)], n = 5)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                         pricepercent winpercent
Nik L Nip                       0.976   22.44534
Nestle Smarties                 0.976   37.88719
Ring pop                        0.965   35.29076
Hershey's Krackel               0.918   62.28448
Hershey's Milk Chocolate        0.918   56.49050
```


:::
:::

The five most expensive candies are Nik L Nip, Nestle Smarties, Ring Pop, Hershey‚Äôs Krackel, and Hershey‚Äôs Milk Chocolate.
Among these, Nik L Nip is the least popular (lowest winpercent).
